Name: Doug Flutie
Fun Fact: On November 23, 1984, with six seconds left and BC down by two, Flutie launched a 48-yard bomb into the end zone. Gerard Phelan caught it. BC beat Miami 47–45. Three weeks later, Flutie won the Heisman.
Notes: 1984 Heisman Trophy winner. #22 retired by BC.

Name: Matt Ryan
Fun Fact: Left Chestnut Hill as BC's all-time leader in passing yards, completions, and touchdowns. In 2016, he led Atlanta to Super Bowl LI, was named NFL MVP, and threw for 4,944 yards. BC's fingerprints all over it.
Notes: #12 retired by BC.

Name: Luke Kuechly
Fun Fact: Made 191 tackles in 2011 — the most by any college player that season and not particularly close. The Panthers took him 9th overall. By age 25, he was NFL Defensive Player of the Year.
Notes: #40 retired by BC.

Name: Mike Ruth
Fun Fact: Won the 1985 Outland Trophy as the nation's best interior lineman — the only BC player to ever win the award. Anchored the same defensive line that powered Flutie's Heisman season. The Giants took him in the second round.
Notes: #68 retired by BC. 1985 Outland Trophy winner.

Name: Andre Williams
Stat Label: Rushing Yards in 2013 (16th in NCAA History)
Fun Fact: Only the 16th player in NCAA history to rush for 2,000 yards in a single season. Took home the 2013 Doak Walker Award, was a Heisman Trophy finalist, and was named ACC Player of the Year — the most decorated single season by a BC running back since the Flutie era.
Notes: 2013 Doak Walker Award. 2013 ACC Player of the Year. #44 at BC.

Name: B.J. Raji
Stat Label: Overall Pick (2009 NFL Draft — Highest BC Defensive Player Ever)
Fun Fact: A Lawrence kid who walked into Chestnut Hill and became the highest-drafted defensive player in BC history. Green Bay took him 9th overall in 2009. In the 2010 NFC Championship, he intercepted a Kyle Orton pass and returned it for a touchdown to put the game away. Two years later, he was a Super Bowl champion.
Notes: #90 at BC. 9th overall pick 2009 (Green Bay Packers). Super Bowl XLV champion. NFC Championship pick-6 was vs. Bears 2010, not in the Super Bowl itself.

Name: Brian Gionta
Fun Fact: BC's all-time leading goal scorer with 123 career goals. Went on to captain the Montreal Canadiens, win a Stanley Cup with New Jersey, and play 18 NHL seasons. His #12 hangs at Kelley Rink.
Notes: #12 retired by BC men's hockey. Same number as Matt Ryan — different sport.

Name: Joe Mullen
Fun Fact: Grew up playing on frozen ponds in Hell's Kitchen before finding his way to Chestnut Hill. Became the first American-born player in NHL history to score 500 goals. Inducted into the Hockey Hall of Fame in 2000.
Notes: #21 retired by BC. Hockey Hall of Famer.

Name: Johnny Gaudreau
Fun Fact: Went by 'Johnny Hockey' for good reason — 80 points in 40 games as a junior, then the 2014 Hobey Baker Award to confirm it. Died on August 26, 2024, alongside his brother Matthew, the night before Matthew's wedding. BC retired his number immediately. The hockey world still hasn't recovered.
Notes: #13 retired by BC post-humously. Died August 26 2024 with brother Matthew in cycling accident.

Name: David Emma
Fun Fact: Emma won the 1991 Hobey Baker Award — college hockey's Heisman — becoming the first Hockey East player ever to claim it. His #16 was the first number BC men's hockey ever retired, the original banner hanging at Kelley Rink.
Notes: 1991 Hobey Baker Award. First jersey retired in BC men's hockey history. #16.

Name: Chris Kreider
Fun Fact: Won two national championships at BC before the Rangers drafted him 19th overall in 2009. A decade later he scored 52 goals in a single NHL season — the most ever by a former BC Eagle. One of the most successful NHL careers the program has produced.
Notes: #19 at BC. 19th overall pick 2009 (NY Rangers). Two NCAA titles at BC.

Name: Brooks Orpik
Fun Fact: Won the 2001 NCAA title at BC, then spent 17 seasons as one of the most respected shutdown defensemen in the NHL. Won the Stanley Cup with Pittsburgh in 2009 and again in 2016. Over 1,000 NHL games, all on the back of four years in Chestnut Hill.
Notes: #44 at BC. 2001 NCAA champion. Stanley Cup 2009 and 2016. 18th overall pick 2000 (Pittsburgh Penguins).

Name: Megan Keller
Stat Label: Career Points by a Defenseman (BC All-Time Record)
Fun Fact: BC's all-time leader in career points by a defenseman — men's or women's — with 158. Alternate captain of Team USA at the 2026 Milan Olympics, where she won gold. Her #4 hangs at Kelley Rink.
Notes: #4 retired by BC women's hockey. 2026 Olympic gold medalist. Record is specifically as a defenseman — Carpenter holds overall scoring record with 278 pts.

Name: Alex Carpenter
Fun Fact: The daughter of former Bruin Bobby Carpenter rewrote every scoring record BC women's hockey had — 133 goals, 145 assists, 278 points. In 2015 she became the first Hockey East player to win the Patty Kazmaier Award. A decade later she was alternate captain of Team USA, leading them to Olympic gold in Milan. Her chapter at BC is settled.
Notes: BC all-time scoring leader (278 pts). 2015 Patty Kazmaier Award (first Hockey East winner). 2026 Olympic gold, alternate captain Team USA. Daughter of Bobby Carpenter (Boston Bruins).

Name: Cayla Barnes
Stat Label: Olympic Medals with Team USA (silver 2018, silver 2022, gold 2026)
Fun Fact: A shutdown defender who anchored BC's blue line for four seasons before becoming one of the most decorated Olympic defensewomen in Team USA history. Silver in 2018, silver in 2022, then gold in Milan in February 2026 — the win that finally ended the drought against Canada.
Notes: #24 at BC. Three-time Olympic medalist. Gold at 2026 Milan Winter Olympics.

Name: Kelli Stack
Stat Label: Career Points (BC Program Record at Graduation)
Fun Fact: Stack finished her BC career as the program's all-time leading scorer with 209 points — a record later broken by Carpenter — and represented Team USA at the 2010 Vancouver and 2014 Sochi Olympics, winning silver medals both times. A cornerstone of the program's rise to national relevance.
Notes: #16 at BC. Two-time Olympian (2010, 2014). Former BC career scoring record holder (since broken by Carpenter). Women's Beanpot Hall of Fame 2020.

Name: Dana Barros
Fun Fact: Boston-born guard who poured in 2,432 career points in Chestnut Hill before taking his shot selection to the NBA. In 1994–95, he made the All-Star team with the Sixers while shooting 50.7% from three — one of the best shooting seasons in league history.
Notes: #3 retired by BC.

Name: Troy Bell
Fun Fact: Scored 2,632 career points — still BC's all-time record by more than 200 points. The Memphis native was a three-time Big East scoring leader and was selected 16th overall by the Celtics in the 2003 NBA Draft. Every BC scoring record still runs through him.
Notes: #2 retired by BC.

Name: John Bagley
Fun Fact: A blur with the ball — Bagley was one of the most electrifying guards to come through Chestnut Hill before the Eagles ever made the NCAA Tournament as a regular. The Cavaliers took him 12th overall in 1982. BC retired #54 at the same ceremony as Barros in 2017, two guards finally getting their due.
Notes: #54 retired by BC. Jersey retirement ceremony February 25 2017 alongside Dana Barros.

Name: Bill Curley
Fun Fact: Led BC to the 1994 Elite Eight — the program's deepest NCAA run in decades — averaging 19.1 points and 8.2 rebounds in the tournament. San Antonio took him 22nd overall. A Duxbury kid who became the face of BC basketball's best modern era.
Notes: Jersey retired by BC. 1994 Elite Eight. 22nd overall pick (San Antonio Spurs). Big East Rookie of the Year 1991.

Name: Terry Driscoll
Fun Fact: Averaged 13.9 rebounds per game across three seasons — a BC record that has stood for over 55 years. Named MVP of the 1969 NIT after averaging 23.3 points across the tournament. The original BC big man.
Notes: Jersey retired by BC in 1999. BC career rebounding record (1,071 / 13.9 rpg — both all-time records). 1969 NIT MVP. #40 at BC — same number as Gerry Ward.

Name: Gerry Ward
Fun Fact: A 6'4 guard who was an All-American and Academic All-American in his senior year — rare combination. The St. Louis Hawks took him 5th overall in the 1963 draft, one of the highest BC players ever taken. His #40 hangs in Conte Forum alongside his name.
Notes: #40 retired by BC men's basketball. Same number as Kuechly in football and Driscoll in basketball — different eras. 2013 ACC Tournament Legend honoree.

Name: Sarah Behn
Fun Fact: One of the most decorated players in BC women's basketball history. BC retired her #33 — the program's first or one of its first retired numbers on the women's side. Verify stats and hometown before finalizing.
Notes: #33 retired by BC women's basketball. Hometown and exact career stats need verification. needs_review.

Name: Alejandro Bedoya
Fun Fact: Led BC to the 2007 ACC championship — regular season and tournament — and was named ACC Offensive Player of the Year. Then he took that same engine to the national team, earning 60+ caps for the USMNT and playing in the 2014 World Cup. The most internationally decorated player BC soccer has ever produced.
Notes: #16 at BC. 2007 ACC Offensive Player of the Year. 2007 ACC regular season and tournament champion. 60+ USMNT caps. Transferred from Fairleigh Dickinson in 2007. Cap count approximate — confirm current total.
